exemplar.title: Investigating how the nightlife centre in Santos impacts the water quality in the nearby Tagus River Estuary

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating how the nightlife centre in Santos impacts the water quality in the nearby Tagus River Estuary

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Consistent use of technical terminology (eutrophication, Pearsonâs r, EC) and concision.
Clear, logical structure with IB IA headings, numbered sections, and coherent progression.
Presentation is coherent throughout, facilitating reader understanding.
weaknesses: Figure numbering inconsistencies between captions and labels.
Bibliography formatting varies and could be more consistent.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three communication strands reach band 3: structure and organization are sustained, terminology and concision are consistent, and the report is logical throughout. This justifies the maximum score of 3.

strengths: Sharply focused research question with clear independent/dependent variables and location.
Deep literature review linking global waterâpollution issues to local Tagus Estuary context.
Comprehensive explanation of causal connections between nightlife litter and measured waterâquality parameters.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands reach the top band: the research question is coherent and focused; the environmental issue is discussed in depth; and connections are explained comprehensively. This justifies the maximum score of 6.

strengths: Detailed, repeatable field and lab method with replication and clear steps.
Explicit justification of systematic sampling strategy to reduce bias and enable statistical tests.
Comprehensive risk assessment table with graded probabilities, severities and mitigations.
weaknesses: Lacks temporal replication and an upstream control site for baseline comparison.
Probe calibration steps could be more detailed (buffer standards not fully specified).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design and sampling strategy are strong (band 5), with minor gaps in temporal replication and control selection preventing band 6. Risk and ethics are comprehensive (band 6), but holistically the criterion performs at the high end of band 5.

strengths: Evaluation of conclusions links high nitrates to broader eutrophication impacts.
Method evaluation identifies key strengths and weaknesses, such as replication and lack of upstream control.
Meaningful suggestions for future studies (weekend sampling, longitudinal design).
weaknesses: Discussion of alternative explanations for some parameters (pH, EC) is brief.
Improvements section omits detailed plans for probe calibration and sample time-stamping.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusion evaluation and method evaluation reach band 4, while improvements align with band 3. Collectively, the criterion performs in the mid-band, warranting a score of 4.

strengths: All relevant raw and processed data visualised via appropriate tables and four clear scatterplots.
Correct calculation and interpretation of means, SDs, and Pearsonâs r showing significant patterns.
Conclusion directly references statistical values and links findings to the research question.
weaknesses: Omissions of p-values and confidence intervals limit statistical completeness.
Some unit labels and axis descriptors are missing, and one misstatement about conductivity was noted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation fully meets band 6, analysis is correct but omits p-values (band 5), and conclusion is valid yet could discuss outliers more (band 5). Overall, the criterion sits solidly in band 5.

strengths: Application proposal of WWTP upgrades and nanomaterials is well justified by findings.
Solution rationale builds on past success and links clearly to nitrate reductions.
weaknesses: Evaluation addresses cost and logistics but omits stakeholder acceptance and technical feasibility.
Only one weakness considered; broader strengths/weaknesses analysis is incomplete.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Proposal strand meets the top band (3), but solution evaluation is at band 2. The overall performance aligns with band 2, so the final score is 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Technical terms (eutrophication, Pearsonâs r, electrical conductivity) are used consistently; language is generally concise given near-maximum word count, with limited redundancy.

The student relates conclusions to global nitrate pollution, eutrophication and Tagus studies, showing insight.  Yet consideration of pH/EC implications and alternative explanations is brief; evaluation is sound but not fully wide-ranging.

The conclusion directly references r-values and SDs, states whether hypotheses are accepted and links numerical findings to chemical interpretation.  While strong, discussion of nitrate outliers could be deeper; support is nonetheless valid and data-driven.

Means, SD and Pearsonâs r with critical value are calculated correctly and applied to each parameter; patterns and statistical significance are identified.  Minor omissions (no p-values, no confidence intervals) keep the analysis from absolute completeness.

Improvements include sampling after weekend peaks, adding upstream sites and a longitudinal study.  These are meaningful but do not tackle all major weaknesses (e.g., temporal replication of controls, probe calibration accuracy) nor propose significant methodological redesign, so low end of 3-4 band.

A repeatable field- and lab-protocol is provided with numbered steps, equipment list, replication (5 samples Ã 5 sites) and immediate analysis.  It is appropriate for collecting sufficient data on four chemical indicators; nonetheless, absence of temporal replication and upstream control slightly weakens completeness, warranting 5 not 6.

Links between the environmental issue and the research are explained step-by-step: how nightlife litter can leach chemicals, how proximity may influence pH, nitrate, DO and conductivity, and why these parameters were chosen.  The connections are explicit and cover causal mechanisms, fulfilling the descriptor for a comprehensive explanation.

The student formulates a clear, sharply delimited question (âTo what extent does distance â¦ affect water-quality levels â¦ in the Tagus River estuary?â).  It specifies independent and dependent variables, measurable parameters and location, allowing a focused investigation; no vagueness or multiple aims are present.

The student notes one strength (ecosystem restoration) and one weakness (cost/logistics) plus a mitigation idea (NGO funding).  Evaluation covers some but not all relevant pros/cons (e.g., technological feasibility, stakeholder acceptance), so mid-band.
The report follows IB IA structure with clear headings, numbered sections and ToC, sustaining organization through 24 pages; no disjointed sections are evident.

Strengths (replication, multiple parameters) and weaknesses (timing, lack of upstream control) are described; limitations such as single-day sampling are noted.  Discussion stops short of exploring instrument accuracy or confounding variables in depth, fitting mid-band.

The introduction moves from global water-pollution concerns to nightlife-generated solid-waste pollution, citing several studies.  Local context (Tagus Estuary, specific clubs) is described together with historic pollution issues, showing depth and relevance.  This constitutes a genuine discussion rather than a brief outline.

The choice of systematic sampling every ~1.5 km, five replicate bottles per site and justification (reduce bias, enable Pearsonâs test) is explicitly argued.  Minor gaps (no explanation of why five sites or why only surface water) prevent a fully exhaustive justification, hence 5.

A detailed risk table grades probability and severity and proposes mitigation; ethical considerations to minimise habitat disruption are noted.  Coverage is broad and specific, matching the âcomprehensive descriptionâ expectation.
All raw data are tabulated; processed means/SD tables and four scatterplots are presented, so every relevant data set is visualised appropriately and clearly.

A specific solutionâreviving estuary clean-up via WWTP upgrades and nanomaterialsâis proposed and justified with past success and nitrate focus; rationale clearly stems from findings, satisfying the highest band for proposal.
Arguments progress logically from background to planning, results, discussion and application; transitions allow the reader to follow reasoning without confusion, indicating coherent presentation.

annotations.comment: The research question is sharply focused, specifying independent and dependent variables, location and measurable parameters, enabling a coherent investigation.
This literature review situates global waterâpollution concepts and defines point vs nonpoint sources well, providing strong context for the research question.
The link between global nightlife litter and local Tagus estuary pollution is explained clearly, showing causal mechanisms for chosen parameters.
The description of electrical conductivity incorrectly states that low EC suggests higher dissolved substances; in fact, high EC indicates greater ionic concentration.
Consider including calibration procedures as a controlled variable (e.g., probe calibration frequency and buffer standards) in Table 1 to enhance method reliability.
Including an upstream control site would strengthen the sampling design by providing baseline waterâquality data unaffected by nightlife runoff.
Before applying Pearsonâs r, the assumption of normality should be checkedâfor example via ShapiroâWilkâensuring the testâs validity.
Record and report the exact time of sampling at each site to account for diurnal fluctuations in waterâquality parameters.
Probe calibration is noted but buffer standard checks are not specified; add a step to use pH buffer solutions and log calibration results.
The risk assessment table is comprehensive, grading probability and severity and proposing effective mitigations for each hazard.
Ethical considerations note habitat disruption but omit data management and permissions; consider adding steps for sample labelling, data storage and local authority permissions.
Raw data in Table 4 lacks unit labels in column headers, hindering immediate interpretation; include units (e.g., pH, mg Lâ»Â¹, ÂµS cmâ»Â¹).
The worked example of mean and SD formulas is clear and pedagogically effective, reinforcing correct data processing.
The summary table of pH averages and SD is well constructed, displaying all relevant processed data succinctly.
The nitrate averages table shows high variability at 1.70 km; discuss potential causes and consider outlier impact on correlation.
While Pearsonâs r is presented, p-values and assumption checks are omitted; include p-values or confidence intervals to fully support significance claims.
The correlation table lists r-values but lacks indication of statistical significance (e.g., *p*<0.05); annotate r-values with significance markers.
Axes in Graph 2 should explicitly display units and sample size (n = 5) to improve clarity and reproducibility.
Figure numbering is inconsistent: the caption refers to Graph 3, but the plot is labeled Graph 4; please align numbering and captions.
The analysis section ends abruptly (âHowever, theâ); ensure all sentences are complete to maintain logical flow and coherence.
The interpretation of conductivity variability is clear, noting higher SD near the centre and reliability elsewhere; this strengthens the analysis.
The conclusion succinctly answers the research question by summarizing the extent of correlations; it is dataâdriven and well supported.
The evaluation links high nitrates to eutrophication and impacts beyond nightlife, effectively situating findings within broader environmental issues.
The method evaluation notes Thursday sampling but omits upstream controls and temporal replication in recommendations; consider these for robustness.
Proposed future study is noted, but detail on sampling frequency and site selection in a longitudinal design would strengthen the improvements section.
The application linking estuary cleanâup to WWTP upgrades and nanomaterials is well justified by nitrate findings and past project successes.
Evaluation of the cleanâup solution addresses cost and logistics but omits stakeholder acceptance and technical feasibility; consider adding these aspects.
The bibliography is extensive but citation formatting varies; adopting a consistent style (e.g., APA) will enhance clarity and professionalism.
